card.intuition: The centroid of a tree is the node whose removal leaves no subtree larger than n/2. Removing centroids recursively produces a decomposition tree of depth O(log n) — every path in the original tree passes through exactly one centroid of every level, enabling divide-and-conquer on trees.
card.analogy: Bisecting a map by finding the 'balance point' city — removing it splits the country into regions each at most half the original size. Solve problems city by city at each level; the depth guarantee means you only need O(log n) levels.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) for decomposition; problems solved in O(n log n) or O(n log² n) depending on per-centroid work
card.space_complexity: O(n log n) for centroid tree
when_to_use: Count paths with a given sum or length in a tree
Closest pair of nodes satisfying some property
Distance queries where brute force would be O(n²)
gotchas: After finding the centroid, mark it as removed before recursing — otherwise it may be selected again
The centroid of a subtree may not be the centroid of the whole tree — always compute sizes relative to the current component
Store the centroid parent[] array to climb the centroid tree during queries

exercise.prompt: Find the centroid of a tree: the node whose removal leaves all components of size ≤ n/2.
hints: The upward component has size tree_size - subtree[u]. The node is the centroid iff no child subtree AND the upward component both exceed tree_size // 2
exercise.test_code:
# Path graph: 0-1-2-3-4 (centroid = 2)
adj1 = [[] for _ in range(5)]
for u,v in [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)]:
    adj1[u].append(v); adj1[v].append(u)
assert find_centroid(adj1, 5) == 2, 'Path of 5: centroid is index 2'

# Star graph: 0 connected to 1,2,3,4,5 (centroid = 0)
adj2 = [[] for _ in range(6)]
for v in range(1,6):
    adj2[0].append(v); adj2[v].append(0)
assert find_centroid(adj2, 6) == 0, 'Star: centre node is centroid'
print('PASS')
